Can you, um, talk to me about a time when you were responsible for driving a strategic initiative, like, end-to-end, and you didn't have a lot of, like, guidance or definitions, you had to push the whole thing forward yourself.
Um, how did you establish clarity and get, like, alignment and buy-in from other team members or, or cross-functionally?

I think one example of that was when I first joined GrowthBook, and the problem at large was like, we had no marketing, now you need to just market.
And I think a lot of that was working with technical co-founders who didn't know what marketing did, and just knew that they needed marketing.
And so a lot of it was explaining, hey, here's what I would do.
Like first I would do an audit and figure out like, where things are good and where things need to change.

And then setting, putting together a strategy in place, and then really explaining that strategy to the co-founders, and then telling them, hey, this is what I need in order to implement the strategy.

Feedback, it's more, not feedback, but a quick intro about it.
It's an open source, like optimizely and launched darkly together.

And so what I explained, and I think they understood, was that they needed more awareness.
And so a large part of that is, like, how do you build that?
And so I worked, I, like, interviewed everyone to understand, like, how current customers were being found.
And then more importantly, figuring out, like, like, if I was CEO of the company, what would I be working on?
it would be, like, building awareness, whether that's, like, figuring out who the ICP is, and then creating that content so that you can drive awareness.
Or, more importantly, we were looking more for referrals, because...
That was actually where most of the growth was coming from, and we wanted to continue that.

And then that was just one aspect of it.
The second aspect was just building content so that people could find us and understand what we were.

cares about.

think one of the biggest accomplishments was within six months, we had to like take a, basically an application and turn it around into a service and then launch it.
It was basically a web application security service.
And so we took this product, which actually scans application.
and finds issues, but the issue there being is that only security people knew how to use the tool, only security people knew how to understand the results and then deal with it.
And so what we did was we took a services model where we would have someone who would work with clients, actually a lot like how growthx is doing.
And then we also had someone who was technical who would use the tool to run scans against websites and then consolidate them into a report.

I think the biggest part was like first understanding whether or not there was a need, then like launching the product, enabling the product, and then doing that all like in a short period when before I didn't quite understand what like web application security was.
I think the neat thing was that it didn't just like begin and end of that, like once the product was launched, it was then like continuing to work with customers and sales.
folks into understanding, like, oh, okay, now that we've built this, what do you really need, right?
Because we won't really know until we've worked with actual real customers.
And then it turns out that they love the results, but they didn't know what to do with it.
And so they needed to actually get more guidance into figuring out, like, how they needed to explain to the developers to fix those issues.

And so then again, we had to, like, find a product out there in the wild, kind of partner with them to build a tool together that would not only, like, show you where the vulnerabilities were, but show you where in the code it was that developers would then go, oh, okay, let me patch that.

And so kind of working through, like, integrating that, also working on, like, showing, like, management, we need actually more resources to do this, showing them, like, the impact of, like, potential growth.
